cards.title: The Curse of High Dimensionality in Visualization
cards.teaser: A chart can faithfully represent 2 dimensions; 3 with effort; beyond that, projection, faceting, and abstraction are required — and every abstraction loses something.

runs.text: A standard chart plots two variables: one on x, one on y. Every additional variable requires an additional encoding channel — color, size, shape, transparency, faceting. Each additional channel consumes cognitive budget.
The practical limit for human simultaneous decoding:

items.text: 2 channels (x, y):
easy. Scatterplot.
3 channels (x, y, color hue):
manageable. Still readable for most audiences.
4 channels (x, y, color, size):
challenging. Experts can read it; general audiences struggle.
5+ channels in one chart:
almost always a failure mode. The reader cannot track all encodings simultaneously.

runs.text: Beyond 4 dimensions, the solutions are:

items.text: Faceting (small multiples):
show multiple 2D charts, each filtered on one level of a third variable.
Pairs plots:
show all pairwise 2D relationships in a grid.
Parallel coordinates:
show all variables as parallel axes with lines connecting each observation.
Dimensionality reduction:
project to 2D using PCA, t-SNE, or UMAP and plot the result.

runs.text: Each approach reveals different structure and hides different structure. The choice depends on what question you're trying to answer.

There is no chart type that shows 10 variables simultaneously with the accuracy of a scatterplot for 2 variables. Multidimensional visualization is always a set of tradeoffs between completeness and legibility.

Like a high-dimensional machine learning feature space — you cannot visualize 100 dimensions directly; every 2D projection discards 98 dimensions of information and emphasizes the structure visible in those 2.

cards.title: Pairs Plots (Scatterplot Matrices)
cards.teaser: A pairs plot shows every pairwise scatterplot in a grid — the most complete view of multivariate structure for 4–10 variables, at the cost of being dense and requiring a patient reader.

runs.text: A
pairs plot

(also called a scatterplot matrix or SPLOM) creates a grid where each cell [i, j] shows the scatterplot of variable i vs variable j. The diagonal typically shows either a histogram/density for each variable or variable names.
What a pairs plot reveals:

items.text: Which pairs of variables are correlated (linear or nonlinear).
Which variables have unusual distributions (visible on the diagonal).
Which pairs have outliers or clusters.
Which correlations are mediated by a third variable (visible when you trace through three cells).

runs.text: When pairs plots are appropriate:

items.text: 4–10 numeric variables.
Exploratory analysis; understanding the variable relationships before modeling.
Feature selection: identifying correlated features, selecting which pairs to investigate further.

runs.text: Design rules:

items.text: Color-code observations by a categorical variable (class label, cluster assignment) to see if groups are separable in any variable pair.
Keep consistent axis scales across the grid.
A 10-variable pairs plot has 90 scatterplots. It is not a communication chart — it is a tool for the analyst.

explanation.code:
import seaborn as sns
# Pairs plot with categorical coloring
sns.pairplot(df, hue='species', plot_kws={'alpha': 0.5},
             diag_kind='kde')

runs.text: For communication, never show the full pairs plot. Use it to find the 1–2 most interesting variable relationships, then build those as clean individual scatterplots.
Like running SELECT * in SQL during development — it's the right tool for exploration when you don't know which columns matter, but you commit to specific columns before exposing the query to users.

cards.title: Parallel Coordinates Plots
cards.teaser: Parallel coordinates place each variable as a vertical axis and draw a line per observation crossing all axes — it shows multivariate patterns that pairs plots miss, at the cost of legibility with many observations.

runs.text: A
parallel coordinates plot

draws each variable as a parallel vertical axis. Each observation is a polyline that crosses all axes at the observation's value for each variable. The result: each line is one observation's complete multivariate profile.
What parallel coordinates reveal:

items.text: Clusters:
groups of lines that follow a similar path across all axes form visible bundles.
Outliers:
lines that cross other lines irregularly or reach extreme axis positions.
Inversely correlated pairs:
if the lines cross between two axes (X-shape), the two variables are negatively correlated. Parallel lines indicate positive correlation.
Class separability:
coloring lines by class label shows which variable combinations separate classes.

runs.text: Limitations:

items.text: Line crossings
make individual observations impossible to trace with more than ~50 observations. Use color or interactive highlighting.
Axis ordering matters.
Adjacent axes are easier to read than distant ones. Axes that measure related concepts should be adjacent.
Scales must be normalized.
Each axis has its own range. Compare patterns, not absolute values, across axes.

runs.text: Interactive parallel coordinates (in D3 v7, Observable Plot, or Vega-Lite) let the reader brush a range on one axis to highlight corresponding observations on all axes. This dramatically improves usability.

Like a feature importance chart for a decision tree except the lines show how each observation traverses the feature space — the cluster of paths that lead to high outcome is directly visible as a bundle.

cards.title: Heatmaps for Two-Dimensional Tabular Data
cards.teaser: A heatmap encodes a matrix of values as a grid of colored cells — the right tool when rows and columns both carry meaning and the pattern across the full matrix is the story.

runs.text: A
heatmap

visualizes a 2D matrix by filling each cell with a color encoding its value. Both the row and column structure are preserved, and patterns across the full matrix become visible through color gradients.
When heatmaps are the right tool:

items.text: The matrix has inherent row and column structure (not arbitrary ordering).
The pattern across the full matrix matters — not just individual values.
Comparing a variable across two categorical dimensions (e.g., hour of day × day of week, user × feature).
Correlation matrices (previous chapter) — a special case of heatmap.

runs.text: Heatmap design decisions:

items.text: Clustering rows and columns
(hierarchical clustering) groups similar rows and columns adjacently, revealing the block structure. This is almost always better than arbitrary ordering.
Color palette:
use a sequential palette for one-directional values; a diverging palette when a meaningful zero exists.
Cell annotations:
print values inside cells when the grid is small (< 15×15) and exact values matter. Omit for large grids.
Aspect ratio:
cells should be approximately square. Extremely wide or tall cells make the encoding harder to read.

explanation.code:
import seaborn as sns
sns.clustermap(df_matrix, cmap='viridis',
               row_cluster=True, col_cluster=True,
               figsize=(12, 10))

runs.text: Heatmaps where both axes are purely nominal (no inherent order) and clustering produces arbitrary ordering can mislead readers into seeing structure that is a layout artifact. Always report whether clustering was applied and what distance metric was used.

Like a database query plan heatmap in a performance dashboard — the rows are queries, columns are operations, and the color blocks show where time is spent; patterns become visible that per-query summaries would hide.

cards.title: Dimensionality Reduction for Visualization (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP)
cards.teaser: When you have 50–50,000 dimensions, dimensionality reduction projects to 2D so you can plot it — but the projection distorts; what's preserved depends on the algorithm.

runs.text: Dimensionality reduction takes high-dimensional data and creates a 2D (or 3D) projection that preserves some aspect of the high-dimensional structure.
PCA (Principal Component Analysis):

a linear projection that preserves global variance. The first two principal components explain the most variance in the data. PCA is fast, deterministic, and interpretable (each PC is a linear combination of the original variables). Fails to reveal nonlinear cluster structure.
t-SNE (t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding):

a nonlinear projection that preserves local neighborhood structure. Points that are close in high-dimensional space appear close in 2D. The result shows clusters clearly. t-SNE distorts global distances: clusters that appear far apart in 2D may not actually be far apart in high-dimensional space.
UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection):

a more recent nonlinear method. Faster than t-SNE, better at preserving global structure, and more stable across different runs with the same random seed. The 2026 default for most ML visualization workflows.
Critical interpretation warnings:

items.text: The x and y axes of a t-SNE or UMAP plot have no interpretable meaning.
Cluster sizes and distances in t-SNE are not proportional to actual cluster sizes or distances.
Hyperparameters (perplexity for t-SNE, n_neighbors for UMAP) change the visual output substantially.

explanation.code:
from umap import UMAP
reducer = UMAP(n_neighbors=15, min_dist=0.1, random_state=42)
embedding = reducer.fit_transform(X_scaled)
plt.scatter(embedding[:, 0], embedding[:, 1], c=labels, cmap='tab10')

runs.text: A t-SNE or UMAP plot that shows 5 clear clusters is evidence of 5 clusters in the high-dimensional space, but the shape, size, and distance of those clusters in the 2D plot are artifacts of the projection. Do not interpret cluster size or inter-cluster distance.

Like a network diagram rendered with a force-directed layout — the proximity of nodes encodes connection, but the absolute position and distance between disconnected clusters are layout artifacts, not data.

cards.title: Small Multiples for Multidimensional Comparison
cards.teaser: Faceting a chart into small multiples is often more honest than adding a third aesthetic channel — the comparison is positional, which is more accurate than color or size.

runs.text: Small multiples

(also called trellis charts or faceted charts) repeat the same chart for each value of a third (or fourth) variable. Instead of encoding the third variable as color, size, or shape in a single chart, you make one chart per category.
Advantages over aesthetic-channel encoding:

items.text: Position is more accurate than color or size.
Comparing bars across panels is easier than comparing colors in a legend.
No channel saturation.
Each panel uses only 2 aesthetic channels (x, y). Adding a fourth variable creates a fifth panel dimension, not a third channel.
Direct comparison.
The reader moves their eye between panels rather than decoding a legend.

runs.text: Requirements for small multiples to work:

items.text: Shared axes across all panels.
If each panel has its own axis scale, cross-panel comparison is meaningless.
Consistent visual encoding.
The same color, the same chart type, the same baseline in every panel.
Panel order encodes information.
Sort panels by a summary statistic or by the variable being faceted.
Minimalist decoration.
No panel borders, no repeated legends, no per-panel titles beyond the facet label.

runs.text: Practical limits: 4–9 panels in a grid are the sweet spot. More than 16 panels on a single page become hard to scan. More than 25 panels require scrolling or interactivity.
When building small multiples in ggplot2, set
scales = "fixed"
(not
"free"
) unless you have a specific reason. Free scales make cross-panel comparison impossible without explicit effort from the reader.

Like separate dashboard panels per service in Grafana — shared time axis, same metric encoding per panel, and you compare performance by scanning panels rather than decoding a 12-line legend on one chart.

cards.title: Categorical Heatmaps and Calendar Heatmaps
cards.teaser: When both axes are categorical and the cell values form a meaningful matrix, a heatmap with categorical axes shows patterns that would require 365 bar charts to express individually.

runs.text: A categorical heatmap has categorical variables on both axes (not the clustered numeric heatmap covered earlier). The cell color encodes a measure for each combination of row and column category.
Calendar heatmap (GitHub contribution graph model):

day of week (7 columns) × week of year (52 rows). Each cell encodes a metric for that specific day. This shows weekly and seasonal patterns simultaneously — a 52-week × 7-day layout makes Monday-Friday work patterns and holiday dips visible at a glance.
Feature × model heatmap:

rows are models (5–20), columns are features (10–50), cells are feature importance values. Clusters of high-importance features for each model are visible without reading 20 separate feature importance charts.
User × feature heatmap:
rows are user segments, columns are product features, cells are adoption rate. Shows which segments use which features — a pattern impossible to read from 50 separate bar charts.
Design rules:

items.text: Use a sequential palette when the measure is always non-negative.
Use a diverging palette when deviation from zero or from a reference is the message.
Sort both axes by a summary statistic to group similar rows/columns together.
Include cell values for small grids (< 10×10); rely on color for large grids.

explanation.code:
# seaborn: calendar-style heatmap (day × hour)
pivot = df.pivot_table(values='count', index='hour', columns='day_of_week')
sns.heatmap(pivot, cmap='YlOrRd', linewidths=0.5)

runs.text: The GitHub contribution graph popularized the calendar heatmap for developer audiences. The format is now so familiar in developer contexts that it communicates activity patterns with no legend or explanation required.

Like a matrix profiler output showing which combinations of index values are hot — the cell structure directly maps to the cross-product of two categorical dimensions, and color shows which combinations are populated.

cards.title: Feature Importance and Radar Charts
cards.teaser: Radar/spider charts are often used for multidimensional comparison but are consistently outperformed by bar charts — they imply a space between axes that doesn't exist in the data.

runs.text: Radar charts

(also called spider charts) display multiple quantitative variables as axes radiating from a common center. Each observation is a polygon connecting its values on each axis. Multiple observations are multiple overlapping polygons.
Why radar charts are popular:

items.text: They look distinctive and "complete" — the polygon fills a space implying a unified profile.
Sports analytics uses them for player comparison; they're culturally associated with expert analysis.
Each variable has equal visual weight.

runs.text: Why radar charts consistently underperform bar charts:

items.text: The area between axes is meaningless.
The polygon area depends on the axis ordering. Rearranging the axes changes the polygon shape without changing the data.
Comparing two polygons is hard.
Overlapping irregular polygons are difficult to compare; side-by-side bar charts of the same data make every comparison direct.
The circular layout implies a circular relationship.
Variables at opposite sides of the chart look "opposed." No such relationship exists in most data.

explanation.code:
Radar: 6 axes × 2 players = 2 overlapping hexagons. Area comparison is intuitive but wrong.
Bar chart: 6 grouped bars × 2 players = 12 bars. Each attribute is directly comparable.

runs.text: If you are replacing a radar chart with a bar chart and the bar chart is less visually impressive, that's not a reason to keep the radar chart. Clarity beats impressiveness in charts designed to communicate.

Like a pie chart — visually appealing and culturally familiar, but consistently outperformed on comparison accuracy by a simple bar chart; the format creates an aesthetic impression that doesn't correspond to how accurately readers extract information.

cards.title: Network Graphs for Relational Data
cards.teaser: When data is fundamentally about connections — social networks, dependency graphs, citation networks — a network layout reveals structure that any matrix representation cannot.

runs.text: A
network graph
(or graph layout) represents entities as nodes and relationships as edges. Unlike every other chart type in this chapter, it does not encode a measure on an axis — the structure itself is the data.
When network graphs are the right tool:

items.text: The relationships between entities are the primary story, not a property of the entities themselves.
You need to identify hubs, bridges, clusters, or isolated components.
Examples: package dependency graphs, citation networks, social follow graphs, microservice call graphs, knowledge graphs.

runs.text: Common layout algorithms:

items.text: Force-directed (Fruchterman-Reingold, D3 force simulation):
nodes repel each other and edges act as springs. Produces organic, readable layouts for general graphs. Scales to ~500 nodes before becoming a hairball.
Hierarchical (Sugiyama/Dagre):
best for directed acyclic graphs — org charts, build dependency trees, pipeline DAGs.
Circular (hub-and-spoke):
places a central node at the center with leaf nodes arranged around it. Good for star topologies.

runs.text: The hairball problem: above ~200 nodes with dense connections, force-directed layouts produce an unreadable tangle of edges. Mitigations:

items.text: Filter to nodes with degree above a threshold (top-k hubs only).
Cluster by community detection algorithm (Louvain, Leiden) and render the cluster graph.
Use edge thickness to encode relationship weight and remove low-weight edges.

runs.text: Tools: D3 force simulation (interactive web), Gephi (desktop exploration), NetworkX + matplotlib (Python scripting), Cytoscape (biological networks).

If the connections aren't the story, a network graph is the wrong chart. A dataset with a "related to" column that you plot as a network instead of a bar chart is hiding the actual measure behind topology that may be arbitrary.

Like a call graph produced by a profiler — the nodes are functions, the edges are calls, and the structure immediately shows which functions are hot paths and which modules are tightly coupled, in a way that a flat table of call counts never could.

cards.title: Cluster Dendrograms
cards.teaser: Hierarchical clustering produces a dendrogram that shows which observations are most similar and at what distance level they merge — height encodes dissimilarity, not time or quantity.

runs.text: A
dendrogram

is the output of hierarchical (agglomerative) clustering visualized as a tree. Leaves are individual observations; internal nodes are the merge points where clusters join. The y-axis encodes the distance at which two clusters merged — the higher the merge, the more dissimilar the groups.
Dendrograms are most useful when combined with a heatmap in a
clustermap

: the rows and columns of the heatmap are reordered according to the dendrogram leaf order. The result shows block structure — groups of observations with similar variable profiles cluster together visually.
Common use cases:

items.text: Gene expression analysis: genes with similar expression profiles across samples cluster adjacently.
Customer segmentation exploration: reveals natural groupings before fitting a k-means model.
Anomaly detection: observations that join the dendrogram at unusually high distances are candidates for outliers.
Feature correlation: cluster the columns of a correlation matrix to group correlated features for feature selection.

runs.text: Interpreting height correctly:

items.text: A merge at height 0.2 means those two clusters are 0.2 apart in whatever distance metric was used (Euclidean, cosine, correlation distance).
The choice of linkage method (single, complete, average, Ward) changes the dendrogram shape substantially. Ward linkage minimizes within-cluster variance and is the standard default.

explanation.code:
import seaborn as sns
g = sns.clustermap(df_matrix, method='ward', metric='euclidean',
                   cmap='vlag', center=0, figsize=(12, 10))

runs.text: Pitfall: the left-to-right ordering of leaves within a cluster is not unique. A dendrogram can be reflected at any internal node without changing its validity. Two adjacent leaves are not necessarily more similar to each other than to a non-adjacent leaf at the same merge level.

Cutting the dendrogram at a chosen height produces flat clusters equivalent to k-means output — but unlike k-means, you can inspect the full merge history to decide where to cut, rather than committing to k upfront.

Like a git merge history viewed in a tree diagram — each merge node shows when two branches of development converged, and the height of the merge encodes how diverged they were; leaves that merged early were nearly identical, leaves that merged late were fundamentally different.
